stories.headline: Fatal hit-and-run crash closes eastbound I-70 near Havana Street for hours
stories.summary:
A hit-and-run crash killed at least one person and injured another on eastbound Interstate 70 near Havana Street in Denver early Saturday morning. The crash, reported just before 5 a.m., involved four motorists and left one victim dead at the scene. A second person was transported to a hospital with injuries of unknown severity.

Eastbound I-70 was closed at Central Park Boulevard for several hours as investigators worked the scene, reopening at approximately 8:30 a.m. Denver police announced the closure and investigation on social media. The identity of the deceased and the suspect vehicle had not been publicly released as of Saturday morning.

The hit-and-run adds to a series of serious traffic incidents on metro Denver highways in recent weeks. Authorities have not announced any arrests in connection with the crash.

stories.headline: Update: 24 Fire nearly doubles in size to 1,126 acres; Chama Canyon blaze reaches 50% containment
stories.summary:
The 24 Fire burning on Fort Carson near Penrose has grown to 1,126 acres with zero containment as of Saturday afternoon, nearly doubling in size since Friday morning. Fort Carson officials cited high winds and extreme heat as drivers of the fire's expanded activity. No injuries or structural damage have been reported, and Colorado Highway 115 remains closed in both directions between Rock Creek Park and a point six miles north of Penrose.

Approximately 80 homes along the highway corridor north of Penrose remain under mandatory evacuation orders, including a two-mile area around Fremont County Road F45 and Colorado 115. The fire continues to burn east of Colorado 115, according to U.S. Army post officials.

In a separate fire roughly 100 miles to the south, the Chama Canyon Fire in Costilla County has grown to 141 acres but reached 50% containment after firefighters deployed engines, heavy equipment, and aircraft. Mandatory evacuations remain in effect for residents along Costilla County roads L.7 and N.2, approximately 17 miles west of San Luis. Forecasters said a cold front moving through Saturday night should offer a brief reprieve before temperatures climb back into the mid-to-upper 80s later next week.

stories.headline: Aurora weighs Stage I water restrictions for 400,000-plus residents as drought deepens
stories.summary:
Aurora Water is recommending Stage I water restrictions to city leaders, which would limit outdoor watering to two designated days per week and call for a 20% overall reduction in water use. If approved, the restrictions would take effect April 7 and would apply to all of Aurora's more than 400,000 residents. The recommendation comes as the city's water supply at its Park County reservoir is declining despite the Aurora Reservoir itself sitting at 95% capacity.

The proposal follows Gov. Jared Polis's activation of Colorado's Drought Task Force on Tuesday, after statewide snow water equivalent fell to among its lowest levels in more than four decades. Aurora Water spokesperson Shonnie Cline warned that the dry conditions could have cascading effects extending into next year. "This is a wake-up call for everybody in Colorado to really start thinking about how we value water," Cline said.

Stage I restrictions represent the least severe tier in Aurora's multi-stage drought response framework. Aurora Water officials said they hope residents will voluntarily comply before stricter measures become necessary. A city council decision on the recommendation is expected in the coming days.

stories.headline: Denver ties all-time March heat record again Friday; 90-degree temperatures expected Saturday
stories.summary:
Denver tied its all-time March temperature record Friday when Denver International Airport recorded a high of 85 degrees, matching the mark set Thursday and breaking the previous daily record for March 20 of 80 degrees set in 1907. The National Weather Service said temperatures across Colorado's plains and the Interstate 25 corridor were expected to climb into the upper 80s and lower 90s on Saturday, potentially setting additional all-time March records.

Saturday was forecast to be the most extreme day of the heat event, with some Denver-area locations possibly reaching 90 degrees — an extremely rare occurrence for March. Increasing winds were expected to compound fire weather concerns, with the combination of heat, low humidity, and gusts creating critical wildfire conditions across the region.

A cold front was expected to move through Saturday night, dropping temperatures back into the upper 60s to low 70s Sunday. However, forecasters warned that warmth would return quickly, with highs near 80 by Tuesday and potentially mid-to-upper 80s by midweek, raising the prospect of additional record challenges and renewed fire danger.

stories.headline: Hazmat spill closes U.S. 6 near Golden for more than two hours after cement truck collision
stories.summary:
A crash involving a cement truck and a box truck closed westbound U.S. 6 near Golden for more than two hours Saturday morning after the collision triggered a hazardous materials spill. The Jefferson County Sheriff's Office shut down the highway at Johnson Road near Colorado 470, directing drivers to seek alternate routes. The highway reopened after the spill was addressed.

The Jefferson County Sheriff's Office announced the closure on social media, warning motorists of a lengthy delay. No information on injuries was immediately released. The Colorado Department of Transportation coordinated the traffic response during the closure.

stories.headline: Plane crashes near El Paso County rural airport; one person hospitalized
stories.summary:
One person was transported to a hospital Saturday morning after a small aircraft crashed near a privately owned rural airport in El Paso County. The El Paso County Sheriff's Office responded to the crash at 11:09 a.m. near Flying View, close to Springs East Airport in the Ellicott and Calhan area east of Colorado Springs.

The Federal Aviation Administration launched an investigation into the cause of the crash. Further details on the victim's condition and the type of aircraft involved were not immediately available. The incident occurred amid an unusually warm and windy weekend across the region.

stories.headline: Denver nonprofit Thriving Families expands to 20,000-square-foot headquarters in Valverde neighborhood
stories.summary:
Thriving Families, a Denver nonprofit that provides free maternal and newborn support services to approximately 1,000 families annually, has purchased a 20,000-square-foot building at 191 Yuma St. in the Valverde neighborhood for $3.1 million. The organization secured the purchase in May 2024 after receiving a $1 million state grant in July 2023 that required the funds to be spent within a year. The building is three times larger than its previous Golden Triangle space.

The nonprofit is investing $1.9 million in renovations to convert the former veterinary hospital into a facility that includes classrooms, a food pantry, a baby boutique, a commercial kitchen, an outdoor play area, and clinician offices. Philanthropist Michael Staenberg, president of The Staenberg Group, provided free painting and electrical work along with donated artwork, saving the organization an estimated $500,000 to $750,000.

Thriving Families' flagship MotherWise program, which educates expectant and new mothers about newborn care at no cost, has been shown to reduce preterm births by 55%, according to executive director Galena Rhoades. The organization began occupying portions of the building in November 2024 and expects renovations to be fully complete by year's end.

stories.headline: Cheesman Park penthouse lists for $5.8 million, one of Denver's priciest residential offerings
stories.summary:
A 4,000-square-foot penthouse overlooking Cheesman Park and the Denver Botanic Gardens has been listed for $5.8 million, among the highest asking prices for a residential property in Denver. The unit at 1133 Race St. is owned by Dr. Bronwyn Bateman, a pioneering ophthalmologist who was the first woman to chair the Department of Ophthalmology at the University of Colorado School of Medicine and founding director of the Rocky Mountain Lions Eye Institute. Bateman and her husband purchased the penthouse for $820,000 in 1994.

The unit features two private exterior elevators, a 1,800-square-foot private rooftop terrace with mountain and city views, 20-foot windows, and three heated garage spaces. Recent renovations include a new kitchen, new HVAC system, and new flooring. The building, Huntington House, is a boutique 30-unit high-rise and one of the few Denver high-rise penthouses situated directly on a park.

Listing agent Marilyn Kal-Hagan of Compass-Denver oversaw the terrace renovation. Bateman, now mostly retired and working part-time at UCLA, said after nearly 30 years in the penthouse, "the time is right" to sell.

stories.headline: Rockies name Kyle Freeland opening day starter after strong spring training finale
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies have named left-hander Kyle Freeland as their opening day starter for the fifth time in his career when they open the 2026 season against the Miami Marlins on Friday. Freeland capped his spring training with a four-inning, scoreless outing against the Kansas City Royals on Saturday, allowing four hits and one walk while striking out two and throwing 63 pitches. His spring ERA finished at 1.00 across three starts.

Freeland, who made his MLB debut with the Rockies in 2017, went 5-17 with a 4.98 ERA last season in 31 starts. The 32-year-old is the team's only left-handed starter and enters the year under new front-office leadership, including president of baseball operations Paul DePodesta and general manager Josh Byrnes. New pitching coach Alon Leichman, formerly of the Marlins, worked with Freeland throughout spring training.

Colorado wraps up its Cactus League schedule Sunday against the Athletics before hosting the Detroit Tigers in two exhibition games Monday and Tuesday. The Rockies then travel to Miami for the regular season opener. The team has lost more than 100 games in each of the past three seasons and acquired veteran starters Michael Lorenzen, Tomoyuki Sugano, and Jose Quintana in the offseason in an attempt to improve.

stories.headline: Nuggets' Peyton Watson expected to return Sunday against Trail Blazers after six-week absence
stories.summary:
Denver Nuggets guard Peyton Watson is expected to make his return to the lineup Sunday when Denver hosts the Portland Trail Blazers at 2 p.m., according to ESPN's Shams Charania. Watson has been sidelined for more than six weeks with a hamstring strain. His last appearance came February 4 in a double-overtime loss to the New York Knicks.

Watson, 23, is having a breakout season for Denver, averaging career highs of 14.9 points, 4.9 rebounds, two assists, 1.2 blocks, and one steal per game while shooting 49.6% from the field and 41.7% from three-point range in 30.7 minutes over 49 games. His two-way versatility has been a key component of the Nuggets' rotation this season.

Denver enters Sunday at 43-28, currently holding the fifth seed in the Western Conference and tied with the Houston Rockets for fourth. The Nuggets' playoff positioning makes each remaining game significant, and Watson's return bolsters a rotation that has been shorthanded during his absence. Portland sits at 35-36 and is still within reach of the postseason.

stories.headline: Avalanche's third line dominates in Chicago as Colorado cruises to 4-1 win
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche defeated the Chicago Blackhawks 4-1 at United Center on Friday, with the team's third line of Jack Drury, Parker Kelly, and Joel Kiviranta turning in one of the most dominant performances by a forward trio this NHL season despite finishing the night without a point. When Kelly was on the ice at 5-on-5, Colorado out-attempted Chicago 30-4 in shot attempts over 12:28. Drury and Kiviranta posted similarly lopsided possession numbers, each recording an expected goals for percentage above 89%.

Chicago goaltender Arvid Soderblom was the primary reason the third line did not reach the scoresheet, making a series of spectacular saves including a desperation lunge to deny Drury's shot late in the game. The win secured Colorado's place atop the Central Division standings, though Dallas trimmed the Avalanche's division lead to two points with a shootout win of its own.

The Avalanche had previously clinched a playoff spot — the first team in the NHL to do so this season. Colorado now prepares for a high-stakes home matchup against the Dallas Stars at Ball Arena.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers weigh alcohol and marijuana tax hikes to fund new Aurora mental health facility
stories.summary:
Colorado legislators are considering increases to alcohol and marijuana taxes as a potential funding mechanism for a new mental health facility in Aurora. The proposal comes as the state grapples with a $1.5 billion budget shortfall that has forced deeper cuts across multiple agencies. Aurora has been at the center of ongoing discussions about mental health infrastructure following a series of high-profile public safety incidents in the city.

The tax increase proposal is being debated at the Capitol as lawmakers search for revenue solutions that do not draw directly from the general fund. Details on the proposed rates and the expected cost of the Aurora facility were not immediately available. Mental health advocates have argued that Colorado's existing behavioral health infrastructure is insufficient to meet demand, particularly on the Eastern Plains and in the metro area.

The bill is among several revenue-raising measures under consideration this session as legislators attempt to balance the state budget while preserving core services.

weather.notable: About 12 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: I don't like country music, but I don't mean to denigrate those who do. And for the people who like country music, denigrate means 'put down.'

topStory.headline: Trump Threatens to Strike Iranian Power Plants as U.S.-Israel War Enters Fourth Week
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump issued a stark ultimatum late Saturday, warning that the United States would attack and "obliterate" Iranian power plants, starting with the largest, if Iran did not fully reopen the Strait of Hormuz to shipping within 48 hours. Trump posted the threat on his Truth Social platform from his Florida home, hours after Iranian missiles struck two communities in southern Israel near the country's main nuclear research center at Dimona, injuring at least 20 people according to Magen David Adom Israel.

Iran responded swiftly, with its army warning it would target all U.S. energy infrastructure in the region if its own fuel and energy facilities were struck. Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz separately announced that joint U.S.-Israeli military operations would intensify in the coming week and that Israel would continue targeting senior figures within the Iranian regime. A hospital and tourist site in southwestern Iran were also reported damaged from U.S. or Israeli strikes, with at least one child killed, according to Iranian state media.

The escalation came just 24 hours after Trump had signaled a possible drawdown, writing Friday that the U.S. was "getting very close to meeting our objectives as we consider winding down our great Military efforts in the Middle East." Within the same day, his administration confirmed it was sending additional troops to the region and, in a separate move, lifted sanctions on some Iranian oil exports for the first time in decades. The contradictory signals have deepened uncertainty about Washington's long-term strategy in a conflict it launched alongside Israel on February 28.

The Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-fifth of the world's oil and gas supply passes in peacetime, has seen shipping traffic nearly halt since the war's early days. Iran has stated the waterway remains open to all nations except the U.S. and its allies. The head of U.S. Central Command, Admiral Brad Cooper, said Saturday that American forces had significantly degraded Iran's ability to threaten vessels in the strait, citing strikes earlier in the week on underground facilities housing anti-ship cruise missiles, mobile launchers, and radar systems.

The conflict's economic consequences are widening, with oil prices surging and global stock markets declining. Analysts note that while much of the oil traversing the strait is bound for Asia rather than North America, the global nature of oil markets means supply disruptions affect prices worldwide. With Trump's 48-hour deadline now in effect and both sides signaling continued military action, the path toward a negotiated end to the conflict remains unclear.
